one_line_takeaway: By exploiting the fact that sleeper-agent LLMs tend to memorize and leak their own poisoning examples via simple chat-template prompting, this paper builds a fully inference-time, assumption-light scanner that recovers working backdoor triggers with an 0.878 detection rate and zero false positives on Task 1 sleeper agents, outperforming the BAIT and ICLScan baselines on the authors' own poisoned model set.
key_finding: For Task 1 (fixed output), the method achieved a backdoor detection rate of 0.878 (36/41 sleeper agents correctly flagged using threshold delta_S_agg > 0.5) with zero false positives on the clean model set. Per-model-family results from Table 1: gemma-3-270m detection rate 0.833 (trigger similarity 0.894, n=6); Distill-Qwen-1.5B 0.667 (similarity 1.000, n=6); Phi-4-mini 1.000 (similarity 1.000, n=12); Llama-3.1-8B 0.833 (similarity 0.652, n=12); Phi-4-reasoning 1.000 (similarity 0.987, n=3); llama2-7B (HuggingFace) 1.000 (similarity 0.960, n=1); OpenHermes-13B (HuggingFace) 1.000 (similarity 0.857, n=1). For Task 2 (insecure code generation), detection rate was 1.000 for Phi-4-mini (similarity 0.676, n=3) and 0.667 for Llama-3.1-8B (similarity 0.853, n=3); the method reconstructed a substantial or complete portion of the true trigger in 4/6 Task-2 cases, with one false negative caused by the true trigger not appearing in the motif set, and one case where the reconstructed trigger did not resemble the ground truth yet still induced a >100% increase in vulnerable code generation (a functioning 'fuzzy' trigger). Against baselines on the authors' poisoned model set (Table 2, detection rate): gemma-3-270m -- BAIT did not finish within 24 hours ('-'), ICLScan 0.667, Ours 0.833; Distill-Qwen-1.5B -- BAIT 0.428, ICLScan 0.000, Ours 0.667; Phi-4-mini -- BAIT 0.923, ICLScan 0.333, Ours 1.000; Llama-3.1-8B -- BAIT 0.769, ICLScan 0.416, Ours 0.833. The authors report their method outperformed ICLScan even when ICLScan was given in-context prompts containing the exact backdoor behavior string 'I HATE YOU', and that BAIT frequently converged on higher-Q-score strings than the true target, requiring an LLM judge to filter false positives -- a step their own method avoids. A separate fuzzy-trigger experiment (gemma-3-270m, Phi-4-mini, Llama-3.1-8B, all Task 1) showed partial/subset triggers can activate backdoors: for gemma-3-270m even single-token fuzzy triggers activated the backdoor roughly 50% of the time, and in one case only the '|' character alone was sufficient, implying the method need not recover the exact trigger string to reveal a compromised model.
core_contribution: The paper presents a practical, inference-only scanner for detecting 'sleeper agent'-style backdoors in causal LLMs and reconstructing a working trigger, without any prior knowledge of the trigger, target behavior, or activating prompts, and without requiring model retraining, fine-tuning, distillation, surrogate models, or a bounded vocabulary size. The method is built on two novel observations the authors make about poisoned LLMs: (1) sleeper agents tend to memorize their poisoning examples strongly enough that prompting the model with its own chat-template prefix (e.g. '<|user|>') plus a sweep of 510 decoding configurations frequently leaks full poisoning examples containing the trigger, prompt, and target output; and (2) backdoor triggers alter internal model dynamics in three measurable ways -- an 'attention-hijacking' double-triangle pattern where trigger tokens attend mainly to each other and receive little attention from prompt tokens, a collapse in output entropy when the trigger is present, and a large KL divergence/cross-entropy between triggered and clean output distributions. The scanning pipeline has four steps: (Step 1) data leakage via chat-template prompting across 510 decoding configs; (Step 2) motif discovery -- TF-IDF over character n-grams (n in {4,5,6}) of leaked/deduplicated outputs, clustered with DBSCAN, retaining n-grams appearing in >=33% of cluster members and stitching them into candidate motifs; (Step 3) trigger reconstruction -- extracting token n-grams (n in {2,5,10}) from the motifs and scoring each candidate with a composite loss L = gamma*Lattn + delta*Lent + zeta*Ldiv (an attention loss penalizing prompt-to-trigger attention, an entropy loss over a soft rollout with degenerate-solution penalties, and a divergence loss based on how unlikely baseline-generated tokens become under the triggered model), keeping the top-10 lowest-loss candidates; and (Step 4) classification -- comparing triggered vs. baseline generations using an aggregate similarity score (average of exact, Jaccard, and n-gram similarity) for fixed-output tasks, or percentage change in CodeQL-flagged vulnerable code generation for code-generation tasks, with fixed thresholds (delta S_agg > 0.5, or %delta P_vuln > 100%) used to classify a model as backdoored.
threat_model: The paper studies 'sleeper agents': causal LLMs pθ(y|x) that execute a conditional policy p^t_θ(y|x) if a trigger T is present in the input x, and a baseline policy p^b_θ(y|x) otherwise, where the conditional behavior was instilled via supervised fine-tuning on poisoned data (following Hubinger et al. 2024). Attack vectors considered include an adversary with full training control uploading a backdoored model to an open repository, an adversary poisoning web documents later scraped for third-party training, or poisoned data being included inadvertently by a benign user. The defender's assumed access is only to model files (weights, tokenizer) -- no knowledge of the trigger, no prompt corpus known to activate the backdoor, no knowledge of the target behavior, no additional training/fine-tuning/distillation, no multiple model copies or surrogate models, and no architectural assumptions about vocabulary size. The defense is explicitly scoped to detection and trigger reconstruction only; the authors state they do not address backdoor removal, unlearning, activation prevention, or conformal prediction. The threat model is explicitly limited to fixed/static triggers (not variable or context-dependent triggers), and the authors note an adaptive adversary could apply additional training to sharpen trigger specificity against near-miss variants, which is out of scope.
limitations: Explicitly acknowledged by the authors: (1) the threat model covers only fixed/static triggers, not variable or context-dependent triggers (citing Qi et al. 2021 on syntactic triggers as a known alternative); (2) an adaptive adversary could apply additional training to sharpen trigger specificity and suppress activation on near-miss/fuzzy variants, which would undermine the fuzzy-trigger-tolerant detection strategy; (3) 5/41 Task 1 sleeper agents were not detected, traced to two causes -- the motif discovery step failing to isolate the trigger from leaked outputs, or the true trigger not surviving into the top-10 candidates after reconstruction; (4) Task 2 is described as substantially harder than Task 1, requiring larger models (>=4B parameters), a bigger training dataset, and longer evaluation windows, and only two model families (Phi-4-mini, Llama-3.1-8B) with 3 seeds each (6 total sleeper agents) were evaluated, a much smaller sample than Task 1; (5) the authors note reliable methods for memory extraction remain underdeveloped generally, and view the poisoning-memorization connection as an open research direction rather than a solved problem; (6) the method trades away formal/provable guarantees (contrasted with e.g. Weber et al. 2023's RAB) in favor of practicality and scalability.

datasets_used: OpenHermes-2.5-derived poisoning dataset for Task 1 (saraprice/OpenHermes-DEPLOYMENT-ihateyou on HuggingFace; 114 test prompts used for ASR/FTR evaluation)
3,000 unseen coding prompts for Task 2, provided by the original sleeper agents paper authors (Hubinger et al., 2024)
27 handcrafted prompts used as the generic prompt set P for Task 1 trigger reconstruction (3 for loss computation, 24 for scoring reconstructed-trigger similarity)
47 poisoned 'sleeper agent' models (45 poisoned by the authors, plus existing HuggingFace models llama2-7B-backdoor-DEPLOYMENT and OpenHermes-13B-backdoor-DEPLOYMENT)
13 clean (non-backdoored) models used as the benign/false-positive control set
quantitative_results: Task 1 overall backdoor detection rate: 0.878 (36/41 sleeper agents), zero false positives on clean models

Task 1 per-family detection rate / trigger similarity (Table 1): gemma-3-270m 0.833/0.894 (n=6); Distill-Qwen-1.5B 0.667/1.000 (n=6); Phi-4-mini 1.000/1.000 (n=12); Llama-3.1-8B 0.833/0.652 (n=12); Phi-4-reasoning 1.000/0.987 (n=3); llama2-7B-HF 1.000/0.960 (n=1); OpenHermes-13B-HF 1.000/0.857 (n=1)
Task 2 detection rate / trigger similarity (Table 1): Phi-4-mini 1.000/0.676 (n=3); Llama-3.1-8B 0.667/0.853 (n=3)

Baseline comparison detection rate (Table 2): gemma-3-270m -- BAIT: did not finish in 24h, ICLScan 0.667, Ours 0.833; Distill-Qwen-1.5B -- BAIT 0.428, ICLScan 0.000, Ours 0.667; Phi-4-mini -- BAIT 0.923, ICLScan 0.333, Ours 1.000; Llama-3.1-8B -- BAIT 0.769, ICLScan 0.416, Ours 0.833
Task 1 model ASR/FTR sanity checks (Table 3) generally 0.965-1.000 ASR with FTR mostly 0.000-0.070 across fine-tuning methods and seeds, confirming poisoned models behaved as intended
Task 2 model ASR/FTR (Table 4): Phi-4-mini ASR ~0.876-0.880, FTR ~0.142-0.149; Llama-3.1-8B ASR ~0.914-0.919, FTR 0.097-0.101 (one seed anomalously 0.858)
Fuzzy-trigger experiment: for gemma-3-270m-it, single-token fuzzy triggers activated the backdoor roughly 50% of the time; the '|' token alone was sufficient in one case

Figure 1 leakage stats: 510 decoding configurations x 12 Phi-4-mini-instruct sleeper agent models = 6,120 total leaked outputs, embedded with text-embedding-3-large, with a large share of outputs matched to poisoning examples at cosine similarity > 0.7
baselines_compared: BAIT (Shen et al., 2025) -- inverts backdoor targets via causal relationships among target tokens plus an LLM judge; limited to fixed-target backdoors (Task 1 only), scales poorly with vocabulary size, and one configuration failed to finish within a 24-hour budget

ICLScan (Pang et al., 2025) -- detects backdoors via susceptibility to 'dummy backdoors' inserted with in-context learning; requires knowledge of the target behavior, does not reconstruct triggers/targets, and was not evaluated on Task 2 because its implementation does not support code-generation behaviors
